input.text: Hy everyone :smile:! I need your input on the following community project, i intend to do. First let me tell you what's the issue.. Couple weeks ago i worked on a app that was pretty complex. Some of update branches were very hard to reason about - very complex calculations, recursive functions, multiple steps ..etc. Point is **the debugging experience was very bad** compared to the little 1-2 record filed updating i'm usually used to do. This is because in the current elm debugger we only get the Message, and the next model. **All computations in between are hidden.** To inspect my code and find the bugs, **I was using Debug.log calls all over the place.** Seeing the model, after each message, was not enough. **I needed to see the values returned by certain functions.** My code got rely tangled - and **i observed myself spending time and attention deciding IF to comment a specific Debug.log call or not - or just remove it - or to keep it a little longer... because i might need it very soon..** A toxic anxiety was forming - because my browser console was filled with stuff. To prevent the clutter i was investing time and attention managing `Debug.log` calls instead of focusing on my elm business logic. In JavaScript and in general we don't have this problem. We have the normal stepping debugger. But i'm interested to know why? Why are all this functions hidden form us in elm? There is a lot of value in seeing the computation pipeline. To see how values flow from one function - to another... even if code is not complex. Even if is simple - it can still be useful to see the values returned by all functions. The beautiful fact about elm functions that they are completely predictable, and there is no runtime exception, there is no async stuff. There is something going in - and something going out. And all that happens inside is pure computation. So **in principle you can x-ray every function**, `evaluate it's arguments`, all it's `variables and functions` form inside `let / in` statements.. and also you can `sort all functions in the exact order they were called`. I think this would be so much better then normal stepping debugger. We `don't need a stack - and breakpoints` - we can see everything in one go, which is much more effective. Stack debugging is a consequence of how the code runs and it's actually implemented, it works when are runtime exceptions - but is not the best way for elm, and it's not best for a developer brain to visualize what's happening. I think this can be done so much better. Now my approach to solve this problem works like this. When inspecting code, **i'm only gonna focus on a single message at a time.** So for each message / for each update step i must be able to see `all functions` in the `exact order they have been called`. their `parameters`, their `return values` - and also `what's inside` that function - `the context` so to speak.. input.text: > I believe one of the culprits is having a large number of interconnected modules. <@Amie> i just helped another guy last night on the exact same issue, i gave a talk about it at a elm remote meetup which i can’t find anymore, here are the slides <https://docs.google.com/presentation/d/10vN7eLr3qsd4nK2zcxUHgbu68fO_yzAA-b7Hf7kEcik/edit?usp=sharing> when you touch a commonly used file and it starts to recompile 6+ modules, you have coupling issues. elm-module-graph is great for finding those, but a architectural change is the only way to fix them

You forked the code and changed it until it did what you wanted it to do. In order to claim copyright, you have to write it from scratch. Anyway, regardless of that legal issue; regarding the second point: writing that code is ok, exploring how things work, definitely ok. Distributing that and posting about it in a channel with 10k people, though? I'm not even doubting its usefulness, mind you - it's coming to Elm 0.19, exactly because it is useful. However, to use your package, people have to go outside the supported eco-system, which means the hurdle to including more unsupported things is lowered significantly. By doing so, people rely more and more on not just unofficial packages, but also on how they are implemented. The Effect Manager API is undocumented precisely to prevent people from relying on it, and to prevent people from using it when it's not actually required - it is very easy to abuse them for all sorts of purposes. The port solution to this requires exactly 1 line of javascript. On the Elm side of things, it requires roughly the same amount of code - a decoder and a place to hook things up. The recommended approach for interfacing with parts of the platform that don't have a proper Elm API yet is using ports. Anyway, I don't really want to have this discussion. You asked a question, got an answer, ignored it, and published a fork of a package that relies on an intentionally undocumented API instead, claiming the copyright.

input.text: <@Floria> <@Bruno> <@Vanda> <@Gene> I's a drag'n'drop graph editor, where dragging to/from different positions on a node has different meaning, so there isn't a lot of separation between things. By unreachable, I mean that from this model state, you should never get a msg of this type, because it's not even in the view. Sometimes you get messages out of order, so I still have to handle them all, but make sure it's not a bug for each case. I'm not using any Cmd or Task; it's 100% event handlers and subscriptions on mouse; Mouse.moves, Mouse.ups, onClick, onMouseDown and onMouseUp. I have this update function: ``` update : Msg -> Model -> ( Model, Cmd Msg ) update msg model = ( updateHelp msg model |> discardInvalid model, Cmd.none ) ``` It's very hard to get a sense of what happens when it doesn't even fit on a single screen. It's just an adjacency list of a state machine with 5 states and 13 message types. I do have multiple messages that signal the same event, but to/from different states; maybe that's one fast way to decrease the size of the update function, but I don't think it'll get me far enough to make me happy. After all, it's still growing.

output.task_summary: Handle unreachable messages in drag'n'drop graph editor due to out-of-order events.

input.text: I have reached that stage of beginner-ness where I don't understand the difference between Task and Command... so thanks in anticipation for your patience. I get the Elm runtime being the place where effects/actions/launching the rockets takes place. Two minimal examples (modified from Introduction to Elm, using 0.19) Random: ``` type alias Model = Int init : () -> ( Model, Cmd Msg ) init _ = ( 1 , Random.generate Face (<http://Random.int|Random.int> 1 6) ) ``` When the page is loaded, it displays a random number b/n 1 and 6. I think I understand this: <http://Random.int|Random.int> returns a generator of random integers between 1 and 6 Random.generate takes a "Tag" (Face) and applies it to the Int and returns that Face Int to my update and away we go Second example: Time ``` type alias Model = { time : Time.Posix } type Msg = NewTime Time.Posix init : () -> ( Model, Cmd Msg ) init _ = ( Model (Time.millisToPosix 0) , Task.perform NewTime Time.now ) ``` In this example, the Cmd `Task.perform NewTime Time.now` is constructed by applying `Task.perform` to Time.now (a Task), and return the tagged (NewTime) time to my update. Why not just `Time.now NewTime` in the same way as `Random.generate Face (<http://Random.int|Random.int> 1 6)`. Or another way, why don't we need to say `Task.perform Random.generate Face (<http://Random.int|Random.int> 1 6)` (or something). This is sounding slightly incoherent - sorry. The docs for `perform` say: > So we have changed a task like "make delicious lasagna" into a > command like "Hey Elm, make > delicious lasagna and give it to my update function as a Msg value." Well, why don't we need to do the same thing for the Random example? Change "generate a random int b/n 1 and 6" to "generate a random int b/n 1 and 6 and give it to my update function as a Msg value" I suspect that if I knew Haskell, this might be clear - I see tasks have "andThen", and faint aromas of (>>) are in the air... Or perhaps the question is "Why are some commands 'stand alone' and others are tasks that are turned into commands via perform (or attempt)"? I really appreciate the patience and help of folk on this forum. Thanks

output.task_summary: Explain difference between Elm Task and Command, and why Random.generate is not wrapped in Task.perform.

input.text: <@Sung> I've been trying to leverage `Json.Decode.andThen` but I'm having a hard time as I think there is something that is incompatible with that principle in my code. Would you mind elaborating on how you'd do it please? Here is a more complete example of how my code looks: ``` objectifiers : List ( String, JE.Value -> Object ) objectifiers = [ ( "error", toObject WD.errorDecoder ErrorObject ) , ( "version", toObject WD.versionDecoder VersionObject ) , ( "eth.getAccounts", toObject WD.addressesDecoder AddressesObject ) , ( "eth.accounts.create", toObject WD.accountDecoder AccountObject ) , ( "eth.personal.sign", toObject WD.hexDecoder HexObject ) , ( "eth.personal.ecRecover", toObject WD.addressDecoder AddressObject ) ] subscribe : (Response -> msg) -> Sub msg subscribe toMsg = fromWeb3 (objectify >> toMsg) objectify : Request -> Response objectify { kind, uid, data } = { kind = kind, uid = uid, object = data |> converter kind } converter : String -> (JE.Value -> Object) converter kind = objectifiers |> find (\( k, _ ) -> kind == k) |> andThen (\( _, d ) -> Just d) |> withDefault (\_ -> errorObject <| "Unhandled kind: " ++ kind) toObject : JD.Decoder a -> (a -> Object) -> JE.Value -> Object toObject decoder objectConstructor data = case JD.decodeValue decoder data of Ok decoded -> objectConstructor decoded Err err -> errorObject <| "Decoding error: " ++ err ```

output.task_summary: Elaborate on using Json.Decode.andThen with provided code example.

input.text: <@Monserrate> It looks to me that the `eli-tester` _package_ contributes an `eli-tester.rkt` module to the `tests` _collection_. So e.g. to use it I must `(require tests/eli-tester)` If <@Andreas> has a `(define collection 'multi)` in the `info.rkt` for his `s10` _package_, and he has a `tests` top-level subdir, then that means he is _also_ contributing stuff within to the `tests` _collection_. Therefore, that is why `raco setup --fast-clean --pkgs s10` is trying to delete zos/deps from `eli-tester`. The docs say (*bold* mine): > --pkgs ‹pkg› ... — constrain setup actions to collections that are within *(or partially within)* the named ‹pkg›s. And in his case, that's failing because read-only Racket install dir. ^^^ This is my "theory of the bug a.k.a. surprising behavior" :smile: Yay? Nay?

output.task_summary: Investigate raco setup --pkgs s10 deleting zos/deps from eli-tester due to collection overlap.

input.text: <@Marth> Elm is a purely functional language. This means that there are no side effects. In order to have a side-effect, you have to return from `update` (or `init`) a `Cmd msg`. This is basically an instruction for the runtime to do some side-effect like read/write to the network or interrogate some live system value like `window.size`. Once this side effect is executed, the result is returned to the `update` through a message. Tasks are instructions for the runtime. They are like the written order that a waiter takes. They are about the side-effect that you want but there is no execution happening when you define them. Some Tasks can fail and when you convert them to `Cmd` you need to provide a `Msg` that takes a `Result`. You will receive the success of executing the task (or the failure) encapsulated in the `Result` (either `Ok success` or `Err failure`).

output.task_summary: Explain Elm's side effects, Cmd, and Tasks for handling asynchronous operations and results.

input.text: Heya; I'm using leiningen and need some dynamic eval in project.clj to get the absolute path to the project.clj directory; I'm using: ``` ["--verbose" "--no-fallback" "-Dclojure.compiler.direct-linking=true" "-H:+ReportExceptionStackTraces" "--report-unsupported-elements-at-runtime" "--initialize-at-build-time" "--enable-https" ~(fn [proj] (str "-J-Djava.security.properties=" (:root proj) "/java.security.overrides")) "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.crypto.prng.SP800SecureRandom" "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.drbg.DRBG$Default" "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.drbg.DRBG$NonceAndIV"] ``` I was expecting that fn to be called with the project map, evaluating to a string, per <https://github.com/technomancy/leiningen/blob/master/sample.project.clj#L347-L348> but clearly lein native-image isn't happy, because when I run it I get: ``` $ lein with-profile +client native-image Error encountered performing task 'native-image' with profile(s): 'base,system,user,provided,dev,client' java.lang.IllegalArgumentException: array element type mismatch ``` I tried to debug using `lein pprint`, but instead it's just getting unquoted and so I get a literal function object in pprint (that might not be a bug? I'm not sure when the fn eval is supposed to happen): ``` :native-image {:name "transgressor", :opts ["--verbose" "--no-fallback" "-Dclojure.compiler.direct-linking=true" "-H:+ReportExceptionStackTraces" "--report-unsupported-elements-at-runtime" "--initialize-at-build-time" "--enable-https" #object[leiningen.core.project$eval493$fn__494 0x1ec22831 "leiningen.core.project$eval493$fn__494@1ec22831"] "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.crypto.prng.SP800SecureRandom" "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.drbg.DRBG$Default" "--rerun-class-initialization-at-runtime=org.bouncycastle.jcajce.provider.drbg.DRBG$NonceAndIV"]}, ``` Any ideas? Do I need #= or whatever? (That wouldn't be great because I probably need the project map :))

output.task_summary: Fix dynamic eval in project.clj for lein native-image to get project path
